text: were pulled from their boats and herded into the group already gathered. And then the soldiers all drew their swords—cutlasses, not the standard short swords—and the slaughter began. The commander ran his sword up into Elean’s gut, his wife gasping, one hand reaching for the commander’s shoulder, the other clutching at the sword impaling her. She heaved once, blood bubbling up out of her mouth. Then the commander pulled the blade free and turned away before she fell. Something hard and jagged and hot lodged itself in Aurel’s throat. He tried to swallow, but it felt as if he were swallowing glass. Elean crumpled to the sand, chest heaving as she cradled her stomach, and then she stilled. Around her the soldiers cut their way through the villagers with ease. Almost no one resisted. No one had the chance. The butchery ended in moments. The commander surveyed the scene, ordered soldiers to search the huts again, the surrounding trees, the boats. Rickard appeared and sauntered up to his commander’s side. Aurel’s king stared down at Elean’s body, then knelt and pulled a tattered swath of red cloth from his jacket. It fluttered in the breeze from the ocean, the gold oak leaf clear. He wadded up the cloth and grabbed Elean’s limp arm, closing her fingers around it. They must have still been warm, her blood still fresh. Then Rickard placed her arm back onto the sand—staged it—and stood. The shard rotated so the scene was no longer in view. Aurel faced Rickard. “You killed Elean.” His voice ground out in splinters. Rickard stiffened in defiance. “The mirror lies.” “Not here,” Aurel said. The crackle-crunch sounded again, louder this time, closer, and Utak’s eyes widened, the whites stark against her skin. “Aurel!” But Aurel was focused on Rickard. He took a step closer. “You did it to frame Ergant, to force his hand. Did you even know Elean was there?” The shards flared, but Aurel didn’t need to look to know the truth. “No, you chose Istabol because Elean was there. Because you knew I’d be destroyed, that I wouldn’t let it go . That’s why you didn’t write down Istabol on your list—because you knew it wasn’t Ergant’s fault. You knew I wouldn’t be able to leave it alone, that I wouldn’t let such an ‘oversight’ slide. You wanted me angry, off-balance. And that’s why you didn’t bring your sword. I was to be your weapon.” “Do you think Istabol was safe?” Rickard asked. “It could have been attacked by Ergant’s pirates as easily as any of the other villages. It could have been next! He needed to be stopped.” “He would have been. Eventually.” “He needed to be stopped now ! Before his marauders hit a more important target.” Aurel stilled and the crackle-crunch filled the silence, a regular sound now, steady, like the slow approach of footsteps, feet shuffling through broken glass. “More important than Elean?” Aurel asked. Realization dawned on Rickard’s face, but Utak had stepped up to Aurel’s side. In a hard whisper, she said, “Aurel, look.” He glanced toward where she nodded, already knowing what he would see. Figures were approaching, mere shadows among the gray in the thickness of the mirror, but edging closer. Shadows made of shards and splinters and ground glass. Shadows with empty black pits for eyes, a tiny spark at their center. Shadows that had already closed half the distance between them. Behind him, Ergant sucked in a hollow breath and stepped toward the other three. “What are they?” “The R’skall ,” Utak said. “The residents of this realm,” Aurel added. Utak faced him, terror in her eyes. “They are unhappy. They want blood.” Aurel glanced down at the slice the shard had cut into his arm, at Rickard’s hands and knees. He met Rickard’s eyes. “They shall have it.” “No,” Rickard said. Aurel motioned to Utak. “Get Ergant out of here.” Utak snatched at Ergant’s hand, already mumbling the incantation. Her gaze met Aurel’s as she stepped to the side, pulling Ergant with her, both of them vanishing into thin air, replaced by one of the R’skall not ten paces away. Others weren’t far behind, closing in on all sides. “You can’t do this,” Rickard hissed in fear. “No blood can be shed in this room!” “We are in a liminal space, both within the room and outside of it. The rules of the Forum don’t apply here.” Rickard’s began shaking. His face transformed into rage. “I am your liege! You swore fealty to me !” “And you swore to protect those who served you.” Aurel began the incantation. Rickard spun to face the R’skall hemming him in, mere steps away. “No,” he heaved. “You can’t leave me here! You can’t abandon me —!” But Aurel stepped to one side, Rickard still in view, his scream building into a howl of pure terror and pain as the R’skall reached for him with jagged arms, enfolding him, glass piercing skin, gouging into organs. Blood spurted. Flesh flensed from muscle and bone. Aurel felt one of the R’skall snag his own arm, the pain frigid and hideous, and then that world grayed and he fell backwards, the R’skall’s hand shredding skin and muscle from upper arm to elbow before letting go. He stumbled backwards out of the mirror, lower back slamming into the black oak table. His left arm shrieked in agony, but he ground his teeth together, breath harsh, and staggered upright. Blood dripped from his fingers and pieces of glass stuck out of his tattered flesh and torn shirt. Each shard burned with cold. Utak took a step forward from where she waited, but halted at the sight of his arm. “They touched you.” “It’s nothing. Our healers will deal with it when I return.” Already, the pain had diminished to a deep throb, his arm going numb with the cold. “You must get every splinter of glass out,” Utak warned. But Aurel ignored her, focused on Ergant,
